citation: Abed V, Fine R, Fine R, Conley C, Jacobs C, Stone AV. Increased Snap Counts Were Not Seen Prior to Lisfranc Injuries in the National Football League. Cureus. 2022;14(12):e32736.
abstract: This retrospective study examined 21 NFL players who sustained Lisfranc injuries during regular season games between 2013 and 2021 to determine whether increased snap counts contributed to injury risk. Player data including position, draft information, snap counts, approximate value (AV), injury timing, and foot laterality were collected from public online resources and the Pro Football Reference database. Injured players were matched to 32 controls based on position, age, BMI, seasons played, pre-injury AV, and snaps per game. Results showed that players played significantly fewer snaps on the day of injury compared to their season average (33.9 vs. 50.3 snaps; p<0.001), and there was no significant difference in career snap rates between the injury year and prior seasons (p=0.20). The majority of injuries (76.2%) occurred during the first eight games of the season, and no significant differences in return-to-play statistics were found between injured players and controls.
tldr: Lisfranc injuries in NFL players are not preceded by elevated snap counts or cumulative workload spikes, suggesting they are acute traumatic events rather than overuse injuries. Most occurred early in the regular season, which may point to pre-season preparation as a modifiable risk factor. Offensive and defensive linemen were disproportionately affected (42.8%), likely due to higher BMI and frequent foot-planting maneuvers. RTP rates and post-injury performance were comparable to matched controls, offering reassurance for roster planning.
methods: NFL players with Lisfranc injuries (2013-2021) were identified by cross-referencing public injury reports, press releases, and game summaries, verified by at least two sources. Inclusion required the injury to occur in-game and after at least one prior season. Snap counts and AV were retrieved from Pro Football Reference. A control group (n=32) was matched to the injured cohort (n=16 with RTP data) on position, age, BMI, pre-injury seasons, AV, and snaps per game. Statistical analysis used Wilcoxon rank-sum tests for skewed variables and Welch two-sample t-tests for continuous outcomes; significance set at p<0.05.
findings: 21 players met inclusion criteria; 18 (85.7%) underwent surgery. Players played significantly fewer snaps in the injury game vs. season average (33.9 +/- 21.9 vs. 50.3 +/- 15.8; p<0.001). No significant difference in career vs. injury-year snaps per game (45.7 +/- 17.1 vs. 50.3 +/- 15.8; p=0.20). Most injuries (76.2%) occurred in the first half of the season. Injury timing within games was most common in Q2 (38.1%) and Q4 (33.3%). Left foot injured twice as frequently as right (66.7% vs. 33.3%). No significant differences between injured players and controls for post-injury seasons, AV, or snaps per game.
limitations: Injury identification relied on public databases, so unreported cases would be missed. Surgical techniques, physical examination findings, and rehabilitation protocols could not be assessed without medical records. Sample size was small (n=21). Acute-chronic workload ratios could not be calculated from available data.
practicalImplications: Lisfranc injuries appear to be acute events not driven by workload accumulation, so snap count monitoring alone is unlikely to prevent them. Early-season injury clustering suggests pre-season conditioning and gradual return to full practice loads may be relevant mitigation strategies. Linemen require particular attention given their disproportionate injury rate.
athleteDev: Offensive and defensive linemen had the highest Lisfranc injury rates, likely related to high BMI and repetitive foot-planting demands. Footwear selection, field surface type, and pre-season conditioning programs targeting foot and ankle strength may be worth evaluating as preventive measures for linemen.
rtp: 85.7% of injured players required surgery, yet post-injury performance metrics (AV, snaps per game, seasons played) were not significantly different from matched controls. Most players (81.3%) returned to their same team. These data support optimistic RTP counseling after Lisfranc injury and surgery, with expected return to pre-injury performance levels.

citation: Wise P, Gallo RA. Increasing Incidence of Pectoralis Major Ruptures in NFL Players. Orthop J Sports Med. 2019;7(7 suppl 5):2325967119S00396.
abstract: This study examined the incidence and characteristics of pectoralis major ruptures in NFL players from 2010 to 2017 using publicly available reports from ESPN.com and NFL.com. Injury details including age, position, injury setting, PED suspension history, and NFL Combine performance data were collected and compared to position-matched peers. Over the eight-year period, 51 players sustained 55 total pectoralis major ruptures, representing a dramatic increase from only 10 complete ruptures reported in the NFL from 2000 to 2010. Defensive players accounted for 82% of injuries, and 87.3% required surgery. Return to play was achieved in 85.4% of athletes who had prior playing time. Combine metrics (bench press, arm length, shuttle time) for injured players were not significantly different from peers, and only 5.9% had prior PED suspensions.
tldr: Pectoralis major ruptures in the NFL have increased more than five-fold over comparable time periods, affecting predominantly defensive players at an average age of 26. The cause of this rise remains unexplained and appears unrelated to baseline strength, agility, arm length, or PED use. Over 85% of injured players who had prior playing time returned to game action, suggesting favorable RTP prognosis with surgical repair. Medical and strength staff should be aware of this rising injury trend, particularly for defensive linemen and linebackers.
methods: Public injury reports from ESPN.com and NFL.com were searched to identify pectoralis major injuries in NFL players from 2010 to 2017. For each injury, details about age, position played, injury setting (game vs. practice), PED suspension history, and NFL Combine performance (bench press reps, arm length, 20-yard shuttle) were recorded. Combine data were compared to position-matched peers at the same workout to assess relative performance. A prior study reported 10 complete ruptures from 2000 to 2010 for historical comparison.
findings: 51 NFL players sustained 55 pectoralis major ruptures from 2010 to 2017 (vs. 10 from 2000 to 2010). Defensive players comprised 82% (42 of 51) of injuries. Mean age at rupture was 26 +/- 1 years. 76.4% of ruptures occurred during games. 87.3% required surgery. Of those with prior playing time whose rupture occurred before the 2017 season, 85.4% returned to play. Injured players ranked at the 63rd percentile for bench press, 50th for arm length, and 52nd for 20-yard shuttle vs. position peers. Only 5.9% (3 of 51) had prior PED suspensions.
limitations: Injury identification depended on public reporting, which may undercount cases. Definitive surgical treatment could not be confirmed for 7 of 55 injuries. The study was descriptive without a formal control group. The mechanism of the incidence increase could not be determined from available data.
practicalImplications: The dramatic rise in pectoralis major ruptures in the NFL warrants attention from strength staff, athletic trainers, and team physicians. Given that relative strength and PED use do not explain the trend, other factors such as training methodologies, practice load, or equipment changes may warrant investigation. High RTP rates support surgical intervention when indicated.
athleteDev: Defensive players (82%) are at highest risk for pectoralis major rupture, likely due to the demands of hand-fighting, shedding blocks, and pass-rushing techniques that place the pectoralis under eccentric load. Monitoring bench press technique and progressive loading during in-season and off-season strength programs may help identify at-risk athletes.
rtp: 85.4% of NFL players with prior playing time returned to game action following pectoralis major rupture, most requiring surgical repair (87.3%). Medical staff should anticipate a prolonged recovery timeline but can counsel athletes and teams on favorable return-to-play prognosis. Monitoring post-RTP performance is warranted given the high-demand nature of defensive positions.

citation: Wang Z, Feng Z, Lu M, Sun J, Liu T, Zhang Q, Wang X. Independent effects of mental fatigue and drop height on drop jump performance in American football athletes: an exploration of central-peripheral interaction. PeerJ. 2026;14:e20574.
abstract: This study investigated the independent and interactive effects of mental fatigue (MF) and drop height on drop jump (DJ) performance in 12 male collegiate American football athletes using a within-subjects repeated-measures design. MF was induced via a 45-minute Stroop task, and DJ performance was tested at drop heights of 30, 40, and 50 cm using a Vicon 3D motion capture system and Kistler force platforms. Outcome measures included jump height, peak vertical ground reaction force (vGRF), rate of force development (RFD), average power output, reactive strength index (RSI), reactive strength ratio (RSR), and lower limb stiffness (Kleg). MF significantly reduced average power output, RSI, and RSR across all drop heights, suggesting impaired central nervous system efficiency. Increasing drop height above 30 cm significantly reduced jump height, RFD, and RSI, while increasing Kleg. No significant interaction between MF and drop height was found, indicating their effects operate through independent mechanisms.
tldr: Mental fatigue impairs power output and reactive strength in American football athletes during drop jumps, independent of drop height, while drop heights above 30 cm reduce jump performance metrics and increase leg stiffness. These findings suggest that cognitively demanding pre-training or pre-game activities (film study, video games, poor sleep) may meaningfully reduce athletes' explosive lower-body output. For applied staff, monitoring athletes' mental fatigue status before plyometric training sessions is warranted, and 30 cm may represent the optimal drop height for American football-specific DJ training given the sport's multidirectional demands.
methods: Twelve male American football athletes (age 22.2 +/- 1.9 years, height 181.4 +/- 3.1 cm, mass 80.1 +/- 8.5 kg) from Soochow University participated. MF was induced using a 45-minute computerized Stroop task; cognitive and effort workload were confirmed via NASA-TLX before and after. DJ tests were performed at 30, 40, and 50 cm drop heights under both baseline and MF conditions in a 2 x 3 repeated-measures ANOVA design. A Vicon infrared 3D motion capture system (8 cameras, 100 Hz) and dual Kistler force platforms (1000 Hz) captured kinematic and kinetic data. Outcome variables were calculated from force-time data and motion capture. Greenhouse-Geisser corrections and Bonferroni post-hoc tests were applied as needed; alpha = 0.05.
findings: MF induction was confirmed by significant increases in NASA-TLX cognitive demand (p<0.001) and effort (p=0.002). MF significantly reduced average power output (p<0.001), RSI (p=0.001), and RSR (p=0.001) across all heights. Drop height significantly affected jump height (p<0.001), RFD (p<0.001), RSI (p=0.001), and Kleg (p=0.002): 40 cm and 50 cm yielded lower jump height, RFD, and RSI but higher Kleg compared to 30 cm. No significant MF x height interaction was found for any variable. Jump height and vGRFpeak were not significantly affected by MF alone.
limitations: Small all-male sample (n=12) from a single institution limits generalizability. Drop heights were restricted to 30-50 cm, limiting extension to other heights. MF was assessed primarily via subjective workload scale without physiological markers. The control condition used no intervention rather than an active low-demand control task, which may limit internal validity.
practicalImplications: Applied sport science staff should consider scheduling high-intensity plyometric training when athletes are not mentally fatigued from prolonged cognitive tasks such as film study, meetings, or electronic device use. Pre-session fatigue screening (e.g., NASA-TLX, reaction time tests) could help identify days when plyometric volume or intensity should be reduced. Brain endurance training may be a viable strategy to mitigate MF effects.
athleteDev: For American football athlete development, 30 cm drop height appears to produce the most favorable combination of jump height, RFD, and RSI, aligning with the sport's demand for multidirectional explosive movements rather than single-direction maximum force. Coaches should avoid excessively high drop heights in DJ training and manage cognitive load on plyometric training days to maximize neuromuscular adaptations.
rtp: Mental fatigue-induced reductions in average power output, RSI, and RSR suggest that athletes returning from injury may be at risk for suboptimal neuromuscular performance if cognitively fatigued during RTP testing or training. RTP readiness assessments should account for the athlete's cognitive state; DJ testing performed after mentally demanding activities may underestimate true lower-limb explosive capacity.

citation: Isenmann E, Veit S, Flenker U, Lesch A, Lachenmeier DW, Diel P. Influence of short-term chronic oral cannabidiol application on muscle recovery and performance after an intensive training protocol - a randomized double-blind crossover study. J Int Soc Sports Nutr. 2024;21(1):2337252.
abstract: This three-arm double-blind randomized crossover study investigated the effects of short-term chronic cannabidiol (CBD) application on muscle recovery and performance in 17 well-trained athletes following a six-day high-intensity training protocol combining strength and interval running sessions. Subjects received 60 mg of CBD oil, CBD solubilisate, or placebo after each training session, with four-week washout periods between arms. Performance was assessed via countermovement jump (CMJ), 1-RM back squat, 1-RM bench press, and 1-mile run before and after the training week. Biomarkers for muscle damage (CK, myoglobin), inflammation (IL-6, IL-10), oxidative stress, and immune cell activity were measured. Athletes were classified as advanced (n=8) or highly advanced (n=9) based on relative squat performance. CBD oil was associated with reduced myoglobin concentration in advanced athletes (p=0.049), but no clear effects of either CBD product on performance parameters were found.
tldr: Short-term chronic CBD supplementation (60 mg/day) during an intensive training week produced minimal measurable benefits for muscle recovery or performance in well-trained athletes. CBD oil modestly reduced myoglobin in advanced athletes but had no impact on performance metrics or inflammatory markers. Critically, performance-level classification (advanced vs. highly advanced) was the strongest predictor of how athletes responded to the training protocol. For applied staff considering CBD supplementation protocols, current evidence does not support its routine use for recovery during high-load training blocks, and performance level should be considered when evaluating supplementation efficacy.
methods: 17 trained male athletes completed a three-arm randomized double-blind crossover trial with 4-week washout periods. The six-day training protocol included whole-body strength training on days 1, 3, 5 (back squat, bench press, lunges, dips, rows at 65-70% 1RM) and high-intensity interval running on days 2, 4, 6 (4 x 4 min at max effort). CBD oil (sublingual, 60 mg), CBD solubilisate (oral, 60 mg), or placebo was consumed after each session. Performance (CMJ via OptoJump, 1-RM squat and bench press, 1-mile run) and blood biomarkers (CK, myoglobin, IL-6, IL-10, OxLDL, immune cell ratios) were measured pre- and post-training week. Linear mixed-effects models were used for analysis.
findings: Both performance levels showed significant CK increases post-training (p=0.002-0.003). Myoglobin increased significantly only in advanced athletes under placebo (p=0.001); CBD oil significantly reduced this increase (p=0.049). CBD solubilisate did not reduce myoglobin. CMJ decreased significantly in advanced athletes under all treatments (p=0.015); highly advanced athletes showed a trend toward back squat decline. No significant changes in IL-6, IL-10, oxidative stress, or NLR were observed. PLR decreased in advanced athletes under placebo (p=0.047); CBD oil showed a trend toward inhibiting this decrease (p=0.083). No significant treatment effects on any performance parameter.
limitations: Eight subjects dropped out due to COVID-19, creating treatment order bias (highly advanced athletes disproportionately received CBD-solu first). Small group sizes (n=8-9 per level) limit statistical power. Blood CBD concentrations were not measured, so bioavailability differences between oil and solubilisate could not be quantified. The washout period (4 weeks) may have been insufficient given persistent adaptation effects observed in biomarkers.
practicalImplications: Applied staff should not expect meaningful recovery benefits from 60 mg/day CBD supplementation during high-intensity training blocks based on current evidence. The finding that athlete training status significantly modulates recovery responses highlights the need to individualize supplementation strategies. Future research should use longer washout periods, measure CBD plasma levels, and specify athlete performance classification to enable more definitive conclusions.
athleteDev: The training protocol (18 work sets per muscle group per week combined with HIIT running) reliably induced measurable muscle damage and performance decrements, confirming its utility as a physiological stress model. The differential response between advanced and highly advanced athletes to the same protocol underscores the importance of individualized training loads in football athlete development programs.
rtp: The study demonstrates that even well-trained athletes experience measurable muscle damage and CMJ decrements following high-volume training blocks; CBD supplementation does not meaningfully accelerate recovery of these performance markers. For RTP contexts, functional performance metrics like CMJ should be monitored after high-load training phases, and recovery timelines should not assume supplementation will accelerate readiness.

citation: Krause DA, Dueffert LG, Postma JL, Vogler ET, Walsh AJ, Hollman JH. Influence of Body Position on Shoulder and Trunk Muscle Activation During Resisted Isometric Shoulder External Rotation. Sports Health. 2018;10(5):407-412.
abstract: This quasi-experimental repeated-measures study examined the effects of body position on external rotation (ER) torque and electromyographic (EMG) activation of shoulder and trunk muscles in 25 healthy overhead recreational athletes performing resisted isometric shoulder ER in three positions: standing, side lying, and side plank. Surface EMG was recorded from the infraspinatus, posterior deltoid, middle trapezius, multifidi, and bilateral internal and external obliques, normalized to maximal voluntary isometric contraction (MVIC). ER force was measured via handheld dynamometer. No significant differences in ER torque were found between positions, but significant EMG differences were found in 7 of 8 muscles. The side plank position produced significantly greater EMG activity in the infraspinatus, middle trapezius, and nondominant external and internal obliques compared to standing and side lying, supporting its use as an integrated shoulder and core exercise.
tldr: When performing shoulder external rotation strengthening, the side plank position produces greater activation of the rotator cuff (infraspinatus) and trunk muscles (obliques) compared to standing or side lying, without sacrificing ER torque output. For football athletes requiring both shoulder stability and core integration - particularly quarterbacks, linemen, and athletes in overhead rehab - the side plank ER exercise is the preferred position to simultaneously target shoulder and trunk musculature. All three positions adequately stimulate shoulder girdle muscles above the 50% MVIC threshold needed for strength gains.
methods: Twenty-five healthy overhead recreational athletes (9 men, 16 women; ages 22-31) participated in this IRB-approved study. Surface EMG electrodes (Bagnoli DE-3.1, Delsys) were placed on the infraspinatus, posterior deltoid, middle trapezius, multifidi, and bilateral external and internal obliques. A 5-second MVIC was recorded for each muscle to normalize signals. Participants performed resisted isometric shoulder ER in standing, side-lying, and side plank positions in randomized order, with ER force recorded via MicroFET 2 handheld dynamometer. Three trials per position were performed with 20-second rest between trials and 2-minute rest between positions. Peak EMG within a 100-ms window was used. Repeated-measures ANOVA with Bonferroni corrections (alpha = 0.05) analyzed differences.
findings: No significant difference in ER torque between positions (standing: 29.5 +/- 10.0 N-m; side lying: 29.3 +/- 11.7 N-m; side plank: 29.3 +/- 10.0 N-m). Side plank produced significantly greater EMG than standing and side lying for: infraspinatus (mean difference vs. standing: 8.12% MVIC, p=0.02; vs. side lying: 13.11% MVIC, p=0.02), middle trapezius (vs. standing: 12.64%, p=0.01; vs. side lying: 11.09%, p=0.01), nondominant external oblique (vs. standing: 44.85%, p<0.01; vs. side lying: 43.39%, p<0.01), and nondominant internal oblique (vs. standing: 22.46%, p=0.03; vs. side lying: 36.43%, p<0.01). Only the side plank position exceeded the 50% MVIC threshold for trunk muscles.
limitations: The sample was young, healthy, and athletic, limiting generalizability to injured, elderly, or post-operative patients. The unexplained finding of greater infraspinatus and posterior deltoid EMG in side plank without increased torque may reflect EMG cross-talk from teres minor. The study examined only isometric ER and did not assess dynamic movement conditions.
practicalImplications: Clinicians and strength staff designing shoulder rehabilitation or injury prevention programs for overhead athletes should consider the side plank ER position when the goal is to simultaneously activate both rotator cuff and core musculature. For purely shoulder-focused strengthening, any of the three positions is adequate. Progression from standing to side plank ER represents a logical continuum that increases the core demand without reducing the shoulder training stimulus.
athleteDev: For football athletes - particularly quarterbacks and receivers who depend on shoulder stability and rotational core strength - integrating side plank ER exercises into shoulder prehabilitation and strengthening programs can address both shoulder and trunk stability simultaneously. This exercise is practical for team settings and progresses naturally from more basic shoulder ER variations.
rtp: The side plank ER position is recommended during the transition phase of shoulder rehabilitation before return to throwing or overhead activity, as it integrates trunk stability with rotator cuff strengthening. Given that all three positions achieved sufficient EMG stimulus for shoulder girdle strength gains, standing and side-lying ER remain appropriate early in rehabilitation when core demands need to be minimized, with progression to side plank as tolerance improves.
